past_medical_history: Occasional palpitations over the past year, never evaluated
History of exercise intolerance over the past 6 months, attributed to deconditioning
Iron deficiency anemia treated 2 years ago, resolved
family_history: Mother has mitral valve prolapse, otherwise healthy
Maternal uncle had a stroke at age 42, etiology unknown
No family history of sudden cardiac death
No family history of cardiac tumors or Carney complex

social_history.exercise: Previously active soccer player, stopped 4 months ago due to increasing dyspnea and lightheadedness on exertion
social_history.living_situation: Lives in university dormitory with roommate

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented x4. GCS 15. Appropriate affect, mildly anxious. No confusion or post-ictal features. Speech fluent without dysarthria. Recall 3/3 objects at 5 minutes.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: CN II-XII intact. Pupils 3mm bilaterally, reactive. Visual fields full to confrontation. No nystagmus. Facial symmetry preserved. Tongue midline.
neurological_exam.motor: Strength 5/5 in all extremities bilaterally. No pronator drift. No fasciculations or atrophy.
neurological_exam.sensory: Intact to light touch, pinprick, vibration, and proprioception in all extremities.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric throughout. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin normal bilaterally. No dysmetria. No intention tremor.
neurological_exam.gait: Normal gait and tandem walk. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: No meningeal signs. No orthostatic vitals performed yet. Cardiac examination notable for a grade 3-4/6 harsh systolic ejection murmur loudest at the left upper sternal border, radiating to the neck. Right ventricular heave palpable. Jugular venous pressure elevated at approximately 10 cm H2O. No peripheral edema. No cyanosis or clubbing.

patient.chief_complaint: Two episodes of sudden loss of consciousness over the past 3 weeks, the most recent occurring today during a pickup basketball game
patient.history_present_illness: A 22-year-old male university student presents to the emergency department after collapsing during a pickup basketball game approximately 45 minutes prior to arrival. His roommate, who witnessed the event, reports that the patient suddenly went pale, appeared to 'lock up' with brief bilateral upper extremity stiffening lasting approximately 5-8 seconds, and then fell to the ground. There was no tonic-clonic activity, tongue biting, or urinary incontinence. The patient regained consciousness within 30 seconds and was confused for approximately one minute before returning to his baseline. Emergency medical services were called and the patient was transported to the ED. The patient reports a similar episode three weeks ago while climbing stairs rapidly in his dormitory building. During that episode, he felt a sudden 'rushing' sensation in his chest followed by tunnel vision, then woke up on the landing with a bruise on his forehead. He did not seek medical attention at that time, attributing it to dehydration and stress from exams. Over the past 4-6 months, the patient has noticed progressive exercise intolerance, becoming short of breath during activities that previously did not trouble him. He stopped playing competitive soccer approximately 4 months ago because he could not keep up. He also reports occasional palpitations described as 'fluttering' in his chest, particularly with exertion. He denies any episodes of syncope at rest, chest pain, or orthopnea. He denies any recent viral illness, fever, weight loss, night sweats, or joint pains. He has not traveled recently and denies any tick exposures. The referring emergency physician was concerned about new-onset seizures given the witnessed 'stiffening' and has referred the patient for neurological evaluation.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 10 Hz alpha rhythm, well-formed and symmetric
background.reactivity: Normal reactivity to eye opening and closing

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: No change in background. No epileptiform discharges provoked. Patient became mildly dyspneic during hyperventilation, test abbreviated at 2 minutes.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Symmetric photic driving response. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Normal awake and drowsy EEG. No epileptiform discharges identified. Wicket spikes noted as a benign normal variant during drowsiness. The abbreviated hyperventilation due to patient dyspnea is noted but not clinically significant for EEG interpretation. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Hyperventilation abbreviated due to patient dyspnea. Routine 30-minute recording; prolonged monitoring or ambulatory EEG may be considered if clinical suspicion for seizures remains high.

findings: Right ventricular hypertrophy pattern: tall R wave in V1 (8 mm), deep S wave in V5-V6
ST-segment depression in leads V1-V4, up to 1.5 mm
Occasional premature ventricular complexes (2 noted during recording)
ecg.interpretation: Sinus tachycardia with right axis deviation and right ventricular hypertrophy pattern. ST-segment depression in V1-V4 with T-wave inversions in V1-V3 may reflect right ventricular strain. Occasional PVCs noted. No primary arrhythmia or conduction block identified on this recording.

valves.Pulmonic valve: Valve leaflets not directly involved. Severe subvalvular obstruction by mass. Peak RVOT gradient 90 mmHg.

findings: RVOT mass: Mobile pedunculated mass, 21 x 18 mm, attached to the right ventricular outflow tract near the pulmonic valve annulus
Interatrial septum: Intact, no PFO or ASD by color Doppler
output.impression: Abnormal values: RV size Severely dilated  (H), RV systolic pressure (estimated) 108 mmHg (H), RVOT mass Mobile pedunculated mass, 21 x 18 mm, attached to the right ventricular outflow tract near the pulmonic valve annulus  (H), Pulmonic valve Valve leaflets not directly involved. Severe subvalvular obstruction by mass. Peak RVOT gradient 90 mmHg.  (H), Tricuspid regurgitation Mild-to-moderate  (H), Right atrium Moderately dilated  (H), Pericardial effusion Small circumferential  (H).

events.description: Frequent premature ventricular complexes: 342 total over 24 hours (1.4% burden), predominantly RVOT morphology (LBBB pattern with inferior axis)
Three couplets of PVCs, no sustained ventricular tachycardia
No significant pauses (longest R-R interval 1.6 seconds during sleep)
Persistent right axis deviation and RVH pattern throughout recording
Patient symptom diary: one episode of palpitations correlated with a PVC couplet while climbing stairs

findings: Frequent premature ventricular complexes: 342 total over 24 hours (1.4% burden), predominantly RVOT morphology (LBBB pattern with inferior axis)
Three couplets of PVCs, no sustained ventricular tachycardia
No significant pauses (longest R-R interval 1.6 seconds during sleep)
Persistent right axis deviation and RVH pattern throughout recording
Patient symptom diary: one episode of palpitations correlated with a PVC couplet while climbing stairs
output.impression: 24-hour Holter monitoring shows sinus rhythm with frequent PVCs of RVOT morphology (342/24h, 1.4% burden). Three PVC couplets but no sustained ventricular tachycardia. No bradyarrhythmias or significant pauses. Persistent RVH pattern. The PVC burden alone is unlikely to explain syncope, but the RVOT origin of ectopy is notable in the context of a known RVOT mass. No primary arrhythmia captured that would independently account for the syncopal episodes.

findings.region: Right ventricular outflow tract, attached to the anterior free wall of the RVOT by a narrow stalk, projecting into the subpulmonic region
findings.signal: T1: Isointense to myocardium; T2: Heterogeneously hyperintense; STIR: Hyperintense; Post-gadolinium: Heterogeneous enhancement with areas of non-enhancement suggesting internal hemorrhage or necrosis; Perfusion: Moderate vascularity
Late_gadolinium_enhancement: No LGE in the RV free wall or septum

output.impression: Well-circumscribed, pedunculated, heterogeneously enhancing mass (22 x 19 x 17 mm) in the RVOT causing near-complete subpulmonic obstruction. Signal characteristics favor a benign myxomatous tumor (cardiac myxoma) given T2 hyperintensity, heterogeneous enhancement, pedunculated morphology, and smooth borders. No features of malignancy (no invasion, no LGE in adjacent myocardium). Right ventricular dilation with mildly reduced RV function secondary to chronic outflow obstruction. No evidence of ARVC (no fatty infiltration, no RV wall motion abnormalities beyond those related to mass effect). Clinical correlation recommended.

output.query: cardiac syncope young adult intracardiac mass RVOT myxoma

results.title: Right Ventricular Outflow Tract Myxoma: A Rare Cause of Exertional Syncope
results.summary: Case of an 11-year-old boy with syncope caused by a 19x19 mm RVOT myxoma causing severe RV outflow obstruction. Exertional syncope was the presenting symptom. Successful surgical excision with complete resolution.

results.title: Primary cardiac tumors: 18 years of surgical experience
results.summary: Retrospective review of 38 primary cardiac tumors. Myxoma accounted for 63%, most commonly in the left atrium (77%). Right-sided myxomas represented 15% of cases and tended to present later with more hemodynamic compromise.

results.title: Cardiac myxoma mimicking epilepsy
results.summary: Report of two patients initially diagnosed with epilepsy whose convulsive episodes were ultimately attributed to cardiac myxoma causing cerebral hypoperfusion. Both had normal EEG. Echocardiography was diagnostic.

results.title: Intracardiac masses: differential diagnosis and approach
results.summary: Review of intracardiac mass differential diagnosis including thrombus, myxoma, lipoma, and non-myxomatous neoplasms. Emphasizes role of cardiac MRI for tissue characterization. Myxomas are T2 hyperintense with heterogeneous enhancement.

output.summary: Right ventricular outflow tract myxomas are rare but important causes of exertional syncope, particularly in young patients. They can mimic seizures due to convulsive movements during cerebral hypoperfusion. Key diagnostic features include exertional triggers, normal EEG, ECG signs of RVH/strain, and elevated BNP. Echocardiography is the initial diagnostic modality, with cardiac MRI providing tissue characterization. Surgical excision is curative with low recurrence rates.

findings.value: Mild lightheadedness at 15 min, resolved spontaneously

quantitative_data.Symptoms during test: Mild lightheadedness at 15 min, resolved spontaneously

interactions: No significant interactions with patient's current medications (albuterol, multivitamin)
warnings: Monitor aPTT every 6 hours during infusion
Hold heparin 4-6 hours prior to planned surgical excision per cardiothoracic surgery protocol
Risk of heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT): monitor platelet counts
Mass may fragment with anticoagulation, though risk is low for pedunculated myxomas
alternatives: Enoxaparin 1 mg/kg SC q12h if surgery delayed (but hold 24h pre-op)
No anticoagulation if surgery is imminent (within 24 hours)

findings.description: EXERCISE STRESS TEST TERMINATED EARLY - Stage 2 Bruce protocol
Reason for termination: Patient developed presyncope with pallor and BP drop to 85/50 mmHg at peak exercise
Peak heart rate achieved: 145 bpm (73% of age-predicted maximum)
Baseline BP 106/64, peak BP 85/50 mmHg (hypotensive response to exercise)
ST depression 2.0 mm in V1-V4 at peak exercise (right heart strain pattern)
Frequent PVCs during recovery phase (8 PVCs in first 2 minutes of recovery)
Recovery: Symptoms and BP normalized within 5 minutes of cessation

output.impression: Markedly abnormal exercise stress test. The patient developed exertional presyncope with hypotensive response at submaximal exercise. No further exercise testing should be performed until the underlying structural abnormality is addressed.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Cardiac syncope due to right ventricular outflow tract myxoma

differential.key_features: Exertional syncope, systolic ejection murmur at LUSB, RVH on ECG, elevated BNP, mobile pedunculated mass on echocardiography, characteristic MRI signal
Brief convulsive movements (convulsive syncope) were misinterpreted as seizure. Normal EEG, no post-ictal confusion >1 min, normal prolactin, exertional trigger all argue against
RV dilation and PVCs of RVOT origin could suggest ARVC. However, no fatty infiltration on cardiac MRI, no LGE, and presence of a discrete mass with obstruction explain findings
Intracavitary mass differential includes thrombus, but enhancement pattern on MRI, pedunculated morphology, and young age without thrombotic risk factors argue against
Exertional trigger without typical vasovagal prodrome. Negative tilt table test. RVH on ECG and elevated BNP inconsistent with benign vasovagal mechanism
Right heart strain on ECG could suggest PE, but normal D-dimer, chronic nature of symptoms, and intracardiac mass on imaging exclude this

optimal_actions.action: Perform 12-lead ECG to evaluate for arrhythmia or conduction abnormality
optimal_actions.expected_finding: RVH pattern, right axis deviation, ST depression V1-V4 — unexpected findings prompting cardiac workup

optimal_actions.action: Obtain routine EEG to evaluate for epileptiform activity given referral for 'seizures'
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal EEG with no epileptiform discharges, making seizure unlikely

optimal_actions.action: Order comprehensive metabolic labs including cardiac biomarkers
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Elevated BNP suggesting right heart strain; normal prolactin arguing against seizure

optimal_actions.action: Request transthoracic echocardiogram given ECG abnormalities and exertional syncope
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Large RVOT mass causing severe obstruction — the critical diagnostic finding

optimal_actions.action: Request cardiac MRI for tissue characterization of the intracardiac mass
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Well-circumscribed pedunculated mass with T2 hyperintensity and heterogeneous enhancement, consistent with cardiac myxoma

optimal_actions.action: Search medical literature for RVOT masses and cardiac syncope in young adults
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Literature supporting RVOT myxoma as rare cause of exertional syncope, often misdiagnosed as epilepsy

optimal_actions.action: Recommend urgent cardiothoracic surgery consultation for surgical excision
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Definitive treatment is surgical resection of the myxoma

optimal_actions.action: Restrict patient from all physical exertion pending surgery
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Exercise restriction to prevent further hemodynamic compromise

critical_actions: Obtain ECG and recognize RVH/strain pattern as a clue to cardiac etiology rather than neurological
Obtain EEG to rule out epilepsy before assuming seizure diagnosis
Order echocardiogram when ECG shows unexplained RVH in a young patient with exertional syncope
Recognize that convulsive movements during syncope (convulsive syncope) do not equal epileptic seizures
Recommend urgent surgical consultation for RVOT mass resection
Restrict physical activity given risk of hemodynamic collapse from RVOT obstruction during exertion
contraindicated_actions: Starting antiepileptic drugs without confirmed epilepsy diagnosis
Discharging patient without cardiac workup based on normal EEG alone
Performing lumbar puncture — not indicated and delays cardiac evaluation
Allowing continued physical activity prior to definitive treatment of obstructive cardiac mass
Administering thrombolytics (not a stroke or PE)
Performing exercise stress test before echocardiogram identifies the obstructive mass (risk of hemodynamic collapse)
key_reasoning_points: Exertional syncope with brief convulsive movements was initially misdiagnosed as new-onset seizures — this is convulsive syncope, a common mimicker of epilepsy caused by transient cerebral hypoperfusion
Normal EEG and normal postictal prolactin argue strongly against epileptic seizures
ECG showing RVH pattern with right axis deviation in a young, previously healthy patient is a critical red flag for structural right heart disease
Elevated BNP (285 pg/mL) with otherwise normal metabolic labs points to cardiac etiology rather than neurological
The systolic ejection murmur at the left upper sternal border localizes to the RVOT/pulmonic valve region and should prompt echocardiography
Primary cardiac masses are rare; intracavitary differential includes myxoma (most common benign), thrombus, lipoma, and malignant neoplasms
Cardiac MRI provides tissue characterization: T2 hyperintensity and heterogeneous enhancement favor myxoma over thrombus or fibroma
RVOT myxomas cause fixed outflow obstruction that worsens with exercise, explaining exertional syncope when cardiac output demand exceeds the obstructed capacity
The diagnostic puzzle lies in the initial referral for 'seizures' — the agent must pivot from a neurological to a cardiological workup based on clues in the ECG, physical exam, and lab results

metadata.difficulty_description: Patient referred as 'new-onset seizures' due to convulsive syncope — the initial ECG is abnormal but does not show a primary arrhythmia, and the critical diagnostic finding (RVOT myxoma) is only revealed on echocardiogram, which must be specifically requested. The agent must recognize that exertional syncope with RVH on ECG warrants structural cardiac evaluation rather than purely neurological workup.
